summary: Optimize Java CI pipeline: remove redundant Maven/JaCoCo/Spotless executions and add surefire parallelism
description_text: Problem The Java CI pipeline ( azure-pipelines.java.yml ) has several redundant executions that waste build time: -U  flag forces Maven SNAPSHOT update checks on every build (~1-3 min network overhead) Corrupted JAR cleanup  runs  unzip -t  on every JAR in  .m2/repository  (~1-5 min), also uses hardcoded  /root/.m2  instead of  $(MAVEN_CACHE_FOLDER) Spotless runs 3 times  per module:  apply  +  check  (default) +  check  (validate) — the 2nd and 3rd are redundant JaCoCo runs 4 times  per module: 2x  prepare-agent  + 2x  report  — only 1 of each is needed Surefire has no parallelism  configured — tests run sequentially within each module Changes azure-pipelines.java.yml Remove  -U  from Maven goals, add  -Dspotless.check.skip=true Replace  unzip -t  JAR cleanup with simple  find -size 0 -delete server/pom.xml Spotless: reduce from 3 executions to 1  check  at  validate  phase JaCoCo: remove duplicate  agent  prepare-agent and  report-test  executions Surefire: add  <forkCount>1C</forkCount>  and  <reuseForks>true</reuseForks> Estimated Impact 3-10 min saved per service build  on CI agents Proven safe via local benchmark (identical test results: 23/23 pass, coverage output identical)

summary: Fix pino logger error serialization in auth-proxy
description_text: Problem The auth-proxy's  http2jmsEndpoint.ts  and  tokenEndpoint.ts  both log errors incorrectly with pino: logger.error('Proxy request failed:', error); Pino's API signature is  logger.error(mergingObject, msg)  — when a string is passed as the first argument, the error object (second argument) is treated as an unused interpolation argument and  silently dropped . This means error details (message, stack trace) are never written to the log output. Discovered while debugging a  Proxy request failed  error on QA2 where the log had zero error details, making root cause analysis impossible. Fix Swap the argument order so pino properly serializes the error: logger.error(error, 'Proxy request failed'); This puts the error object in the  err  JSON field with full message + stack trace.

summary: Test case creation in testRail
Fix duplicate DCRelationship sends in user-relationship-enricher integration test
description_text: Problem The integration test  testDCUserRelationshipArrivesBeforeDCRelationship  in  auctions-user-relationship-enricher  was flaky/failing with: Expected 100 records but got 113 and earlier with: Expected 100 records but got 103 Root Cause The test intended to send DCRelationships  after  DCUserRelationships to verify out-of-order arrival handling. However, the code contradicted the comment "Prepare the DCRelationship but DON'T send either yet" by immediately calling  produce()  on both DCRelationships right after creating them. This caused DCRelationships to be sent  twice  — once before and once after the DCUserRelationships. Since the topology uses a KTable-KTable foreign key join (both sides reactive), the second send triggered re-evaluation of all matching DCUserRelationships, producing duplicate output records. Fix Removed the two premature  inboundDCRelationshipProducer.produce()  calls so DCRelationships are only sent after all DCUserRelationships, matching the test's intended behavior.

Auctions: Increase Ram to 4GB per LT session for User Relationship Enricher
description_text: Following the introduction of a foreign key KTable join in the auctions-user-relationship-enricher service, we observed increased memory consumption in our loadtest environment. The service joins DC Relationships with DC User Relationships using a KTable-KTable foreign key join (replacing the previous KStream-KTable join), which requires additional state store memory. This caused out-of-memory (OOM) pod crashes. To resolve the issue, we are increasing memory allocation from 2GB to 4GB and setting fixed replica counts for the loadtest and production environments. Changes: Loadtest: Set memory requests/limits to 4GB, replicas to 4 (min 4, max 4) Production: Set memory requests/limits to 4GB, replicas to 4 (min 4, max 4)

summary: RTS Eligible Items are being removed from view when they receive a Nucleus Reject
description_text: The root cause of this issue is a field name collision between two Avro schemas that are being written to the same MongoDB collection ( xpro-activity-console ) via the  activity-console-bl-row  Kafka connector. Both  InlineRTSAlertUIRow  (from topic  mktx.trading.auctions.moc._protected.rts-inline-order-alert-ui-row ) and  RtsAuctionsEnrichment  (from topic  mktx.trading.auctions.moc._private.client.rts.enrichment-event ) have a top-level field named  auctions , but with different nested structures. When an RTS is created, the enrichment event writes a document with  auctions: { enabled: true/false } . When the RTS is subsequently staged and fails, the alert UI row attempts to write  auctions: { warning: "...", failedRequestId: "...", ... }  to the same document (matched by the same key/document ID). Because the connector uses the  UpdateOneTimestampsStrategy  write model, the second message's  auctions  field  overwrites  the first message's  auctions  field rather than merging them. This causes the alert data to replace the enrichment data (or vice versa depending on timing), effectively making the item "disappear" from the activity console UI since the expected structure is no longer present. To Reproduce: Create an RTS that will fail validation when staged Stage the RTS Observe that the item disappears from the activity console

summary: PI 27 Auctions Market Data Updates
description_text: Objective The goal of this issue is to enhance the Auctions Market Data experience by allowing users to analyze imbalance information and receive market data updates without the need to upload orders. Context Currently, users must upload orders to access market data updates for eligible securities in the Auction. This process is cumbersome and time-consuming, as users need to manipulate CSV files to populate order attributes. The enhancement aims to streamline this process and improve user experience. Scope Included: Direct population of Market Data Attributes within the eligible securities grid. Dynamic market data updates for eligible securities during the auction. Display of CP+ Bid, Mid, and Offer directly in the grid. Not Included: Changes to the SFTP process in Aladdin. Modifications to user permissions regarding data visibility. Success criteria Users can analyze imbalance information without prior order uploads. Market Data Attributes are populated directly in the eligible securities grid. CP+ Bid, Mid, and Offer are displayed for the duration of the session. Imbalance information is updated every 15 seconds during the imbalance phase. Risks and dependencies Potential user confusion regarding the visibility of CP+ data. Dependency on the timely update of market data every 15 seconds. Other information See   for expected behavior. MVP 11/18 Version:  Users must upload an order into a staged state to receive Market Data updates. Issue:  Users currently need to export eligible securities into a CSV, manipulate it, and paste it back to the session, which is cumbersome given the 10-minute time constraint. Action Items: Populate Market Data Attributes directly within the grid. Ensure users receive dynamic market data updates. Clarify visibility of CP+ data and its permissions. Maintain imbalance information on the UI, updating every 15 seconds until the session ends.

summary: PI 27 Auctions Peg Offsetting Orders
description_text: SEE Objective The goal of this issue is to implement a new functionality for Pegged Orders, allowing users to customize their pegged limits based on execution references. Context This feature aims to enhance the user experience by providing flexibility in how pegged orders are executed. It builds on existing Auto-X rules, which users are already familiar with. The implementation will involve changes to the current system to accommodate user-defined execution points. Scope Included: Development of a standardized Pegged order type. Implementation of user-defined execution references for pegged orders. Configuration options for different product segments. Not Included: Changes to other order types outside of Pegged orders. Modifications to the existing Auto-X rules beyond what is necessary for this feature. Success criteria Users can successfully select a pegged order type with CP+ limits. Users can customize their pegged limits based on specific basis points or percentage variations. The system dynamically updates pegged orders based on market conditions. Documentation is updated to reflect the new functionality. Risks and dependencies Dependency on the current matching engine's capability to handle the new pegged order logic. Risk of user confusion if the new functionality is not clearly documented and communicated. Potential delays in implementation if agreement on the approach among stakeholders is not reached. Other information For expected behavior of the functionality, see  . MVP 11/18 Version : One standardized Pegged order type - When Pegged orders are selected, the hard limit on that order is CP+ on the aggressive side. Version 2 Enhancement:  Customize your pegged limit: Allow a user to peg their order against their own execution reference without being required to enter in a numerical value on every order. summary: Performance: Split out MOC connector from default connector
description_text: The current “default” matching connector is handling both general flow and MOC (Market-On-Close)–related “hot” topics, which concentrates traffic on a single connector instance and can create scalability and performance bottlenecks. To improve throughput, isolation, and operability: Split out MOC traffic Identify all MOC-related topics currently processed by the default connector (including auctions or other MOC-specific flows). Create a dedicated  MOC connector  to handle these hot topics separately from the default connector. Ensure configuration (topics, consumer groups, deployment parameters, monitoring/alerts) is clearly separated and documented. Common auctions / low-traffic connector (if appropriate) Analyze existing auctions / low-traffic topics related to MOC and similar flows. Where it makes sense, introduce a  common “auctions / low-traffic” connector  to handle those topics together, rather than overloading the main default connector.

summary: moc-order-view index cleanup
description_text:
The MOC order view ( moc-order-view ) currently includes a filter condition that excludes records where both  status  equals "Dnt" AND  eventType  equals "CancelAccept" at the same time. This filter is implemented using MongoDB's  $match  stage with a  $not  and  $and  combination.

Without proper indexes on these fields, MongoDB would need to perform collection scans to evaluate this filter condition, which can lead to poor performance as the collection grows. Adding dedicated indexes on both  status  and  eventType  fields will allow MongoDB's query optimizer to efficiently filter out unwanted records. Issue created in Slack using    /jira create   .
